meta.title: Kurjun — Archive of Stories
meta.desc: Kurjun — a traditional bag as a metaphor for storing and passing on stories, knowledge and memory.

hero.caption1: Each piece holds a story. Each story continues a life.
hero.caption2: Keeping culture alive through women's hands
hero.desc: Weaving lives, keeping culture alive through Kyrgyz craft

intro.quote: This is a collection of stories of women craftswomen from the Naryn region. Through their work, they create, teach, and shape everyday life around them.
intro.sub: This project explores how women use traditional craft in their everyday lives — to create, work, and shape their communities.

context.p1: The women shared different stories — each shaped by their own experiences, choices, and paths.
context.p2: They use craft in their own ways: to work, to support themselves, to teach others, and to build connections with people around them.
context.p3: Through what they do, they shape their lives, their communities, and what these practices mean today.

s2.title: Toktosunova Kenjekan
s2.quote: "Every Handmade Piece is a Letter to the Next Generation"

s3.title: Moldalieva Nazira
s3.quote: Crafting and Lighting a Way Forward

s5.title: Mustapaeva Nurgul
s5.quote: Learn. Create. Listen to Your Heart

about.heading: About the project
about.body:
"Kurjun is a digital space carrying the stories of Kyrgyz craftswomen. 

 Here, you will find how, through craft, they create, teach, and shape the world around them."
about.col1:
Kurjun is the result of a research project about craftswomen from Kyrgyzstan. 
Here, traditional craft is a space where women create and share, build connections and communities, provide for themselves, and find deep meaning in their making - carrying culture forward through their hands. Their craft is not a fixed tradition; it is something they actively shape. 
For a period of time, Kyrgyz craft was defined from the outside perspective, often looking at it as a static object, while keeping the craftswomen themself outside of this conversations.
about.col2:
While their work was visible, often their voices were not. This platform serves as a response to that. 
A response to the absence of spaces where craftswomen can speak for themselves about their work, their choices, and their lives. We gathered the stories of craftswomen from the Naryn and Ysyk-Kol regions. They are personal stories, but they reflect something much wider about who we are and what we carry with us.

credits.heading: Authors & Acknowledgements
credits.ack1: Kurjun was created by Nurkamal Zhetigenova as part of a UTD Youth Initiative Development Project and diploma research project at the University of Central Asia, under the supervision of Elena Kolesova and Anisa Sabiri.
credits.ack2: Thanks to the support of the University of Central Asia and the UTD Youth Initiative Development Project, whose belief in student-led ideas helped bring this space to life.
credits.ack3: The heart of this project belongs to the women who opened their homes, shared their stories, and trusted us. This space exists because of them.
credits.ack4: To my friends and family — thank you for your patience, your encouragement, and for always being close.
credits.person1bio: Senior student of Communications and Media at the University of Central Asia and the author and researcher of this project. She was responsible for the concept development, fieldwork, interviews, writing, and all photo and visual materials. Her work focuses on storytelling through visual media and research-based creative projects. This project brings together her interest in women's lived experiences, craft practices, and multimedia storytelling.
credits.person2bio: Associate Professor at the University of Central Asia where she teaches Research Methods, Political Communication and Popular Culture in Central Asia and supervises capstone projects. Elena's research crosses cultural studies, media studies, education, cultural anthropology and history. It equally crosses geographical borders from Russian to Japan, on to New Zealand, where she has spent most of her academic career, and finally to Central Asia. She has particular interest in oral history and conducted extensive research about women in creative industries in Central Asia.
